Before turning the phones back on Sunday morning, discuss over coffee how the disconnection felt. Note any improvements in sleep quality or connection to reinforce the habit for the next week.

goal: Reorganize the digital workspace to prevent accidental doomscrolling
description: An entrepreneur's phone is a chaotic mess of work apps and social media. They open their phone to check an invoice or reply to a client and end up watching videos for 40 minutes because the icons are right next to each other. They need to completely separate utility from entertainment.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Only has a few hours on a Sunday afternoon to completely overhaul their system.
baseline.physical_constraints: Experiences decision fatigue quickly when organizing digital files and settings.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must keep LinkedIn and Twitter installed for lead generation, but wants to stop mindless browsing on them during off-hours.

tasks.description: Clear the entire first page of the home screen. Populate it only with boring, highly functional tools: Calendar, Calculator, Maps, Notes, and Banking. This makes the phone act like a tool, not a toy.
Group all social media, news, and entertainment apps into a single folder. Move that folder to the third or fourth page of the home screen, requiring multiple swipes to access.
Go into the phone's settings and ruthlessly turn off notifications (banners, badges, and sounds) for everything except calendar events, texts, and phone calls.
Install the native screen-time tracking widget prominently on the second page of the home screen. This creates a visual point of accountability before swiping further to find entertainment apps.
Delete the native app for the most addictive platform (e.g., Reddit or TikTok) and force yourself to log in through the mobile web browser if you want to use it, adding intentional friction.

goal: Transition from watching hobby videos to actual physical creation
description: An amateur woodworker spends 2-3 hours an evening watching Instagram reels and YouTube tutorials about woodworking, but never actually goes out to the garage to build anything. They feel intimidated by the perfection of online creators and use video consumption as a false sense of productivity.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Only has free time after 8:00 PM when the kids are asleep; garage cannot be excessively loud after 9:30 PM.
baseline.physical_constraints: Often mentally fatigued from a desk job, making the physical barrier to entry feel high.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must use the phone for music/podcasts while working in the garage.

tasks.description: Print out physical blueprints or sketch designs on paper before going to the garage. This eliminates the need to unlock the phone to check measurements, which often triggers a scrolling spiral.
Start the playlist or podcast while still inside the house. Upon entering the garage, place the phone inside a closed toolbox drawer across the room so it is out of sight.
Commit to just 15 minutes of low-stakes physical activity, like sanding a scrap piece or organizing clamps. This overcomes the perfectionism barrier that drives the user back to watching videos.
Once a week, identify and unfollow one top-tier creator whose highly produced content triggers imposter syndrome rather than genuine inspiration.

goal: Reclaim the teacher planning period from passive scrolling
description: A high school teacher uses their 50-minute planning period to 'decompress' by scrolling TikTok, which leads to them bringing grading and lesson planning home every night. They feel overworked and resentful, but their brain craves the dopamine hit after dealing with noisy classrooms.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Planning period is strictly 50 minutes; must share an office with two other chatting teachers.
baseline.physical_constraints: Experiencing vocal fatigue and sensory overload by mid-day.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must check school email for urgent administrative updates during this block.

tasks.description: Spend exactly 5 minutes reviewing the school email inbox for emergencies. Reply to what is urgent, close the email tab, and immediately put the smartphone inside a zipped desk drawer.
Write the three most important tasks (e.g., 'Grade Quiz 4', 'Copy worksheets') on a physical sticky note and place it on the keyboard. Do not open the laptop until the list is written.
Put on noise-canceling headphones playing brown noise or instrumental music to block out the shared office chatter. Work continuously for 25 minutes using a physical desk timer.
Instead of using the remaining 10 minutes to scroll, leave the office without the phone and walk a lap around the quietest part of the school building to physically process the morning's stress.

goal: Prepare and execute a 72-hour strict digital detox for deep writing
description: An academic is severely behind on their thesis deadline because they constantly check Twitter and news sites under the guise of 'research.' They have booked a 3-day weekend alone to finally write the core chapters, but fear their addiction to distraction will ruin the retreat.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Only has 3 days to complete 10,000 words; location is a rented cabin with WiFi.
baseline.physical_constraints: Prone to tension headaches from marathon typing sessions.
baseline.non_negotiables: Requires the laptop for Microsoft Word; must check in with their partner once a day for safety.

tasks.description: Download all necessary PDF journals, datasets, and reference materials into a single local folder on the desktop. This eliminates the excuse of needing the internet to 'look something up.'
Upon arriving at the cabin, physically unplug the WiFi router and lock the power cord in the car trunk. The inconvenience of retrieving it creates a massive barrier to mindless browsing.
Power down the smartphone entirely. Move the SIM card into a pre-purchased basic 'dumb phone' that only has calling and texting capabilities for the duration of the retreat.
Call the partner at exactly 7:00 PM each night for 15 minutes. This satisfies the safety requirement without allowing continuous, day-long text message interruptions.
Keep a piece of paper beside the laptop. Every time the autonomic urge to open a new tab or check a feed arises, make a tally mark. Seeing the physical volume of urges helps demystify the addiction.

goal: Break the cycle of anxiety-driven parenting forum doomscrolling
description: A parent of a toddler is experiencing severe anxiety. They spend hours every evening reading parenting forums, Reddit threads, and milestone-tracking apps, comparing their child to others. This 'research' is intended to soothe anxiety but actually fuels panic and robs them of joyful presence.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Toddler has a speech delay, requiring actual legitimate research and medical coordination.
baseline.physical_constraints: Chronically exhausted, making the couch and the phone the path of least resistance at night.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must use the phone to communicate with the speech therapist via a secure medical app.

tasks.description: Delete all native forum apps (Reddit, Facebook, specific parenting apps). Bookmark only two evidence-based medical websites on the browser, preventing algorithmic 'rabbit holes.'
Restrict all child-development research to a single 20-minute window on Tuesday and Thursday afternoons. If a worry arises outside this window, write it in a notebook to check later.
Replace the evening scrolling habit with 10 minutes of writing in a physical journal. Focus entirely on one positive interaction or funny moment the child had that day, ignoring clinical milestones.
When the chest-tightening urge to Google a medical symptom hits, drop the phone and immediately do 10 wall push-ups or hold a piece of ice. Break the psychological panic loop with physical sensation.

goal: Eliminate smartphone usage while eating meals alone
description: A single professional living alone has developed a habit of propping their phone against a water glass and watching YouTube shorts or scrolling Instagram during every meal. They are eating too quickly, suffering from indigestion, and feeling entirely disconnected from the sensory experience of their food.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Eats all weekday breakfasts and dinners alone in their apartment.
baseline.physical_constraints: Silence in the apartment feels deafening and uncomfortable initially.
baseline.non_negotiables: Can listen to background music, but cannot look at any screens with moving images or text.

tasks.description: Before sitting down with the food, take the smartphone and plug it into a charger in a completely different room (e.g., the bedroom). Return to the kitchen table empty-handed.

If the silence is too anxiety-inducing, turn on a smart speaker or radio to play jazz, classical music, or a long-form interview podcast before sitting down. This provides stimulation without locking the eyes to a screen.
Eat the first three bites of the meal with eyes closed. Focus entirely on identifying the specific spices, textures, and temperatures of the food to anchor the brain in the physical present.

Once a baseline of tolerance for being screen-free is established, introduce a physical magazine or non-fiction book to the table for longer meals. This keeps the mind engaged without the addictive algorithmic loop.

goal: Break the cycle of social media comparison to cure creative block
description: An illustrator uses Instagram to promote their art, but opening the app to post turns into hours of scrolling other artists' work. This triggers severe imposter syndrome, making them feel their work isn't 'good enough,' leading to paralysis and avoiding the drawing tablet altogether.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must post to social media twice a week to maintain algorithm engagement for freelance commissions.
baseline.physical_constraints: Works alone in a home studio, making the phone the only source of 'social' interaction during the day.
baseline.non_negotiables: Cannot delete the app; relies on DMs for client inquiries.

tasks.description: Do not open any social media app until at least one hour of physical or digital sketching is completed. Establish that the brain must produce before it is allowed to consume.
When posting art, set a physical timer for 10 minutes. Upload the image, reply to recent comments, and close the app immediately when the timer sounds, without looking at the main feed.
Unfollow or mute artists whose work triggers anxiety or jealousy rather than genuine inspiration. Protect the digital environment so it serves as a tool, not a weapon against self-esteem.

Instead of using Pinterest or Instagram for reference images, spend time looking through physical art books or taking walks to photograph real-world textures, breaking the reliance on the screen for ideas.

goal: Execute a 30-day social media blackout for a major certification exam
description: A nursing student is one month away from their board exams. They are panic-studying but constantly checking TikTok and Snapchat to self-soothe their intense anxiety, which only fractures their focus and leaves them feeling more unprepared.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must study 6-8 hours a day; exam date is immovable.
baseline.physical_constraints: High caffeine intake and stress are causing poor sleep, lowering daytime willpower.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must coordinate with a study group; needs YouTube for specific medical tutorial videos.

tasks.description: Post a final message on all social platforms explaining the 30-day absence for boards, and provide a phone number for urgent texts. This creates social accountability and relieves the fear of 'ghosting' friends.
Delete the apps from the phone. Change the passwords to a random string of characters, write it on a piece of paper, and give that paper to a roommate to hide until the exam is over.
Install a website blocker (like Cold Turkey or Freedom) on the laptop to block web versions of social media and news sites, while explicitly keeping educational domains whitelisted.
Plan specific, offline activities for 15-minute study breaks (e.g., stretching, petting a dog, making tea) to replace the reflex of picking up the phone when a textbook chapter gets difficult.

goal: Reduce mindless mobile gaming and increase daily mobility
description: An older adult has developed a severe habit of playing 'match-3' and digital slot games on their tablet for 4-5 hours a day. They justify it as 'keeping the brain sharp,' but it has led to a highly sedentary lifestyle, stiff joints, and neglecting household hobbies.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Lives in a climate with harsh winters, making outdoor walking difficult on some days.
baseline.physical_constraints: Mild arthritis makes prolonged standing uncomfortable; eyesight requires a large tablet screen rather than a phone.
baseline.non_negotiables: Uses the same tablet to video call grandchildren and read the morning news.

tasks.description: Move the tablet charger from the side table next to the favorite armchair to a kitchen counter. The tablet must remain plugged in the kitchen overnight and during the day when not actively used for a specific purpose.

If the user wants to play a game, they must do so while standing up at the kitchen counter. If their legs or back get tired, the gaming session must end. This limits sessions naturally without requiring complex app blockers.

Replace one hour of gaming with listening to an audiobook or a nostalgic radio show while slowly pacing the house or doing light stretches. This provides entertainment while stimulating physical blood flow.
Every Sunday, choose one game application that causes the most mindless 'zoning out' and permanently delete it. Slowly shrink the library of available distractions.

goal: Establish strict weekend boundaries for work-related phone checking
description: A mid-level manager is physically present at their children's weekend soccer games but mentally absent. They reflexively check Slack and email every 10 minutes, responding to non-urgent messages and blurring the lines between the office and home, causing resentment from their spouse.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Weekends are packed with family logistics and chores.
baseline.physical_constraints: The phone is used as the primary camera to take pictures of the kids playing sports.
baseline.non_negotiables: Cannot delete work apps because they are required on Monday mornings; must be reachable by text for family coordination.

tasks.description: At 6:00 PM on Friday, drag the work email and team messaging apps off the home screen and into a buried folder. Turn off notifications for these specific apps at the OS level until Monday morning.

During family events (like a soccer game), use the lock screen shortcut to access the camera. Take the photos, and physically lock the phone again without swiping up to see the home screen or notification center.

When at home on Saturday and Sunday, leave the phone on a high shelf in the kitchen. Wear a basic digital watch to check the time, removing the excuse of 'I just pulled it out to see what time it is.'

If weekend checking is driven by Monday anxiety, allow exactly 20 minutes on Sunday night after the kids are asleep to scan the inbox for true emergencies, write a to-do list, and then shut the phone off completely.

goal: Execute a tech-minimalist romantic vacation
description: A couple is going on an anniversary trip. On previous vacations, they spent hours in the hotel room scrolling social media, looking up restaurant reviews, and posting photos, which led to arguments about not being 'present.' They want to travel like it is 1999.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Trip lasts exactly 7 days in an unfamiliar city.
baseline.physical_constraints: Neither partner has a good sense of direction, leading to anxiety about getting lost without GPS.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must use the phone for mobile boarding passes and scanning museum tickets.

tasks.description: Before leaving, print out physical copies of the hotel confirmation, flight details, and a map of the destination city. Circle 3 potential restaurants near the hotel to avoid 'review paralysis' on the street.
Once the plane lands, switch both smartphones to Airplane Mode. Turn on Wi-Fi only for specific, necessary transactions (like calling a rideshare), then immediately revert to Airplane Mode.
Purchase a disposable camera, a Polaroid, or a basic point-and-shoot digital camera. Leave the phones in the hotel safe while sightseeing to eliminate the urge to instantly edit and upload photos.

Allow a strict 15-minute window in the evening, back at the hotel, to connect to Wi-Fi. Use this time to text family that you are safe and check for absolute emergencies. Do not open social media apps.
